did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep neural network (specifically an EfficientNet-B0 model) for skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset. It also employed five post-hoc Explainable AI (XAI) techniques (Integrated Gradients, DeepLift, Gradient, Deconvolution, and Guided Grad CAM) to evaluate the model's interpretability and trustworthiness in the context of dermatology.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training of an EfficientNet-B0 deep neural network on the HAM10000 dataset for skin lesion classification. It reports new quantitative results (accuracy, F1-score, etc.) and performs a comparative evaluation of five Explainable AI (XAI) techniques using specific metrics (Faithfulness, Robustness, etc.) and statistical tests (Friedman test, Wilcoxon post-hoc tests). Although published in a conference proceedings (MMAR), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Comprehensive Quantitative Evaluation of the Performance and Trustworthiness of Deep Learning Models - Skin Lesion Classification Case Study
journal: 29th International Conference on Methods and Models in Automation and Robotics (MMAR)
brief_summary: This study addresses challenges in automated skin lesion classification by evaluating a deep neural network classifier trained on the imbalanced HAM10000 dataset. The researchers utilized an EfficientNet-B0 model pre-trained on ImageNet, employing rigorous cross-validation and data augmentation to mitigate class imbalance. The model achieved noteworthy performance with accuracy, F1-score, precision, and recall values of 77.60±0.67, 70.25±1.09, 72.69±1.28, and 70.20±1.18, respectively. To enhance trustworthiness, five post-hoc Explainable AI techniques (Integrated Gradients, DeepLift, Gradient, Deconvolution, and Guided Grad CAM) were quantitatively evaluated using metrics for Faithfulness, Robustness, Localisation, Complexity, and Randomisation. Statistical analysis confirmed significant differences between the XAI methods, highlighting the importance of rigorous validation for clinical adoption. The findings underscore the need for standardized evaluation protocols and transparent decision-making in AI-assisted dermatology.

lesion_types_studied.details: Most prevalent class in the HAM10000 dataset with 6,705 images in the training set.
Malignant lesion class with 1,113 images in the training set.
Benign lesion class with 1,099 images in the training set.
Malignant lesion class with 514 images in the training set.
Pre-malignant/malignant lesion class with 327 images in the training set.
Benign lesion class with 142 images in the training set.
Benign lesion class with 115 images in the training set.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin lesion categories (melanocytic nevi, melanoma, benign keratosis-like lesions, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma, vascular lesions, dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic images using an EfficientNet-B0 deep neural network on the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) rather than continuous values. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, F1-score, precision, and recall.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (ISIC-2018 Challenge Task 3)
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, sourced from the ISIC-2018 Challenge Task 3. It consists of 450x600 pixel images with accompanying binary lesion segmentation masks manually created by a single dermatologist.

use.training: Used for training the EfficientNet-B0 model with data augmentation (flips, rotation, color jitter, resized crop, affine transformation, perspective transformation).
use.validation: Used for validation during training (early stopping, hyperparameter optimization) and for calculating XAI metric distributions (2003 samples).
use.testing: Used for external evaluation of the model's classification performance (1511 images without masks).
use.other: The training set includes segmentation masks, but the test set does not.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the normality of the distributions of the XAI metric values for each method.
To assess the overall differences between the XAI methods for each metric (Faithfulness, Sensitivity, Localisation, Complexity, Randomisation).

statistical_methods_used.method: Wilcoxon post-hoc tests with Bonferroni correction
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To elucidate pairwise differences between the explanatory methods after finding significant overall differences.
To measure similarity in the Faithfulness metric by comparing model output probabilities before and after feature perturbation.

hypothesis_testing.test: D'Agostino-Pearson test for normality; Friedman test for overall differences between XAI methods; Wilcoxon post-hoc tests with Bonferroni correction for pairwise comparisons.
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: For the Friedman test: There are no differences in the distributions of the XAI metric values (Faithfulness, Sensitivity, Localisation, Complexity, Randomisation) among the five evaluated XAI methods (Integrated Gradients, DeepLift, Gradient, Deconvolution, Guided Grad CAM). For the D'Agostino-Pearson test: The distribution of the metric values for a specific XAI method follows a normal distribution.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: For the Friedman test: At least one XAI method produces significantly different metric values compared to the others. For the D'Agostino-Pearson test: The distribution of the metric values for a specific XAI method does not follow a normal distribution.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The D'Agostino-Pearson test indicated that metric distributions (except Sensitivity for Integrated Gradients) deviated significantly from normality (p < .001 for most), justifying the use of non-parametric methods. The Friedman test confirmed statistically significant overall differences between XAI methods for all metrics (all p < .001). Wilcoxon post-hoc tests with Bonferroni correction showed that most pairwise comparisons between methods reached statistical significance, indicating distinct performance profiles for the XAI techniques.

HAM10000 (ISIC-2018 Challenge Task 3).notes: Balanced accuracy was omitted as it is equivalent to weighted recall in value. The model demonstrates competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.

HAM10000 (Validation Subset).notes: Metrics represent aggregated values for XAI methods. DL achieved the top rank in Faithfulness. GGC performed best in Localisation and Complexity. IG took the top spot in Robustness (Sensitivity). Statistical analysis (Friedman test) confirmed significant differences between methods (p < .001) for all metrics except Sensitivity for IG (p = .24). Wilcoxon post-hoc tests with Bonferroni correction showed most pairwise comparisons were significant.

architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels (after center-cropping 450x600 images to 450x450 and resizing)

architecture_details.selection_rationale: Selected based on efficient scaling and robust performance on dermoscopic images
architecture_details.final_layer_modification: Modified to enable seven class predictions through the implementation of a SoftMax activation function

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (Optuna used for hyperparameter optimization)
training_details.loss_function: Validation loss function (minimized via Tree-Structured Parzen Estimator)
training_details.hyperparameters: Optimized using a hybrid Tree-Structured Parzen Estimator via Optuna (v4.0.0)
training_details.training_schedule: Early stopping mechanism implemented to mitigate overfitting
data_augmentation: Color jitter (brightness, contrast, saturation at 0.1; hue at 0.05)
Affine transformation (translation up to 10%, scaling 0.9-1.1, 10° shear)
training_details.hardware: Intel Core i9-14900K CPU, 32 GB RAM, NVIDIA RTX 4090 GPU (24 GB VRAM)

preprocessing_techniques: Center-cropping from 450x600 pixels to 450x450 pixels to preserve aspect ratio
Color jitter (brightness, contrast, saturation at 0.1; hue at 0.05)
Affine transformation (translation up to 10%, scaling 0.9-1.1, 10° shear)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Brightness, contrast, and saturation adjusted at 0.1; hue adjusted at 0.05.
Cropped to 224 pixels with a scale between 0.8 and 1.0.
Translation up to 10%, scaling from 0.9 to 1.1, and 10° shear.
Applied with a 0.1 distortion scale at a 0.5 probability.

conclusions: The EfficientNet-B0 deep neural network achieved noteworthy performance in multi-class skin lesion classification on the HAM10000 dataset, with accuracy, F1-score, precision, and recall values of 77.60±0.67, 70.25±1.09, 72.69±1.28, and 70.20±1.18, respectively, placing it competitively alongside state-of-the-art methods.

Quantitative evaluation of five post-hoc Explainable AI (XAI) techniques revealed distinct performance profiles: DeepLift achieved the top rank in Faithfulness, Integrated Gradients excelled in Robustness (Sensitivity), and Guided Grad CAM performed best in Localisation and Complexity.

Statistical analysis using the Friedman test confirmed significant differences between the XAI methods across all evaluated metrics (Faithfulness, Sensitivity, Localisation, Complexity, and Randomisation) with p < .001, except for Sensitivity in the case of Integrated Gradients (p = .24).

Wilcoxon post-hoc tests with Bonferroni correction indicated that most pairwise comparisons between XAI methods reached statistical significance, although some pairs (e.g., Gradient and Deconvolution for Complexity) showed less pronounced differences.
No single XAI method excelled in every metric category, highlighting the importance of using multiple evaluation metrics to assess trustworthiness and interpretability in clinical AI applications.

The study underscores the need for standardized evaluation protocols and transparent decision-making to enhance trust in AI-assisted dermatology, particularly given the challenges of dataset imbalance and lesion variability.
important_remarks: The study relies on the HAM10000 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., 6,705 melanocytic nevi vs. 115 dermatofibroma), potentially biasing model performance and generalizability.

The test set (1,511 images) lacks the binary lesion segmentation masks present in the training set, limiting the ability to perform pixel-level ground truth validation for XAI methods on the final evaluation.

Quantitative XAI metrics showed discrepancies with qualitative visual assessments; for instance, Guided Grad CAM had the highest quantitative localization score but produced visually less coherent attribution maps compared to gradient-based methods.

No single XAI method excelled across all trustworthiness metrics (Faithfulness, Robustness, Localisation, Complexity, Randomisation), indicating that relying on a single metric or method is insufficient for comprehensive evaluation.

The EfficientNet-B0 model achieved a weighted F1-score of 78.48% and macro F1-score of 70.25%, which, while competitive, may be limited by the inherent variability and similarity among lesion classes in the dataset.

The study highlights a scarcity of standardized evaluation protocols in existing dermatology AI research, emphasizing the need for rigorous, multi-metric validation to ensure clinical trustworthiness.

Statistical analysis revealed that metric distributions for XAI methods generally deviated from normality (p < .001), necessitating the use of non-parametric tests (Friedman, Wilcoxon) for valid comparisons.
